observations.recommendation: We recommended to require the Human Resource and Management Officer to prioritize the hiring of the Local DRRM Officer II and Local DRRM Assistant positions to ensure comprehensive implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and management as required under RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.

We recommended that BAC Chairperson direct the BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report of the Municipality and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester in accordance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and furnish the Audit Team a printed copy of the PMR to provide basis for monitoring the status of procurement activities of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRM Fund to the Special Trust Fund account amounting to ₱2.257 million, in accordance with Section 21 of RA No. 10121; (ii) conduct regular reconciliations between the Trust Liabilities – DRRM reported in the books of accounts and the actual cash balances to identify and address discrepancies promptly; and (iii) ensure that all future unutilized balances are properly accounted for and transferred at year-end; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to: (i) conduct detailed reconciliation of the transactions affecting the STF to determine the cause of the discrepancy between the SAAOB and the accounting records amounting to ₱415,020.85; and (ii) effect the necessary adjustments where appropriate.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Record the full value of the work accomplished or the gross amount per progress billing under the Construction in Progress account;
Separately account for the liquidation of advances to contractor by debiting CIP account and crediting Advances to Contractor account; and
Adjust prior entry to correct the understatement of CIP and overstatement of advances to contractor.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Direct the Bids and Awards Committee through the BAC Secretariat to submit copies of all contracts/purchase orders irrespective of amounts, and each and every supporting documents forming part thereof to the Audit Team within five working days from the date of execution or issuance thereof as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; and
Instruct the General Services Officer to notify in writing and furnished the Auditor concerned with copies of Acceptance and Inspection Receipt (AIR) within 24 hours after acceptance of deliveries of goods and services in accordance with Item 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002 dated May 18, 2009.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the BAC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to strictly enforce the mandatory posting of warranty security before final payment is made for completed infrastructure projects to guarantee that the contractor shall perform his responsibilities as prescribed in Section 62.2.3.1 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and Section 9.1.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended and the Management agreed the following:
Local Chief Executive to sign all the supporting documents before approving the payment;
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure that all supporting documents such as the purchase order, notice of award, and notice to proceed are completely signed by the LCE before being transmitted to the winning bidder; and
Municipal Accountant to meticulously check on the propriety of supporting documents on every transaction to ensure that complete signatures are supplied thereon especially those that are crucial to the execution of the contracts. Any unsigned documents should be returned and have them signed by the authorized signatories before processing the payment.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
Offices concerned to properly plan their procurement activities through the preparation of the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) which contains all the anticipated materials, services, goods, civil works requirements and the BAC Secretariat to consolidate the same into an Annual Procurement Plan;
BAC to undertake procurement in bulk to save time, costs and manpower hours using public bidding as the primary mode of procurement and discontinue the practice of procuring supplies and equipment through shopping or small value procurement unless the conditions in Section 52.1 and 53.9 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 are met; and
BAC, TWG and Secretariat to avoid/stop splitting of contracts in compliance with the revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the Management agreed in coordination with the GAD Focal Person to:
Continue to maintain the GAD database, enhance and implement the LGU GAD Code and use of gender-monitoring tools like HGDG in compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
Formulate and implement the use of GAD Agenda which shall be the basis for the annual formulation of PPAs to be included in the LGU GPB, and require the alignment of the GAD Plan with the GAD Agenda; and
Maximize the utilization of GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related PPAs in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to undertake the procurement of goods and services beyond the threshold through competitive public bidding as mandated under RA No. 9184 and its IRR. Alternative methods of procurement shall be resorted to only in the highly exceptional cases provided for in RA 9184. In all instances, the procuring entity shall ensure that the most advantageous price for the government is obtained.

We recommended and the members of the Municipal Local School Board agreed to:
Prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization and refrain from accommodating expenditures for programs and activities which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute; and
Prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures shall be in accordance with the provisions of laws, rules and regulations prescribing the limitations on the use of the fund set forth in Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and Item 4.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended and the Management agreed the following:
Refrain from charging expenditures which are for administrative purpose or being used for regular operations of the Municipality, and observe limitations on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund as enumerated in Section 3.2.5 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020;
Instead, focus the fund utilization on developmental projects with long-term/sustainable impact and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the LGU’s constituents; and
Revert to the 20% DF the amount of ₱50,680.00 representing disbursements for non-development projects, which shall be re-appropriated to finance only those developmental programs, projects and activities identified to be funded under the 20% DF for the ensuing year.

We recommended and the Management agreed the following:
Department heads: (i) maintain a separate logbook in respective departments to record time-ins and outs and leaves of employees and submit photocopies of these at the end of each pay period to the HRMO to serve as their check and balance; (ii) ensure that approved leave forms are submitted to the HRMO and a copy retained after submission thereto; and (iii) review all DTRs for inconsistencies and ensure correctness of time and attendance of each employee before signing/approving such DTRs; and
Human Resource Management Office: (i) consider installing an alternative mechanism/system for recording time and attendance of employees in case of system downtime or hardware failure; and (ii) develop and enforce concrete policies for those employees who fail to abide with the policies concerning time and attendance and filing of leaves of absences.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the General Services Office (GSO) to cause the immediate marking of the seven units of motor vehicles with the word “For Official Use Only” (3 inches) and two units bearing the name of the agency operating or using the same as required under COA Circular No. 75-6 and Manual on the Audit of Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles.

We commended the efforts of Management for being compliant with the GSIS law and we recommended that it sustain efforts in the correct deduction of premiums and loan amortizations and in the timely remittance thereof pursuant to the prescribed period under Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No. 8291.

We commended the efforts of Management for being compliant with the HDMF law and we recommended that it sustain efforts in the correct deduction of premiums and loan amortizations and in the timely remittance thereof pursuant to the prescribed period.

We commended the efforts of Management for being compliant with the PhilHealth law and we recommended that it sustain efforts in the correct deduction of premiums and loan amortizations and in the timely remittance thereof within the prescribed period.

We recommended to direct the Municipal Accountant reconcile the balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts under the three funds and book the necessary adjustments to bring their balances into agreement. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should closely monitor transactions affecting reciprocal accounts and ensure that these are recorded in the different funds simultaneously to avoid any error or misstatements in the financial statements.

Strictly utilize and adhere to the guidelines on the purpose of the five percent Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund as outlined under Section 3.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and

Stop the practice of purchasing T-shirts for Local DRRM personnel and responders, and avoid incurrence of expenditures charged against the five percent LDRRMF that are not supportive of disaster risk management activities.

The Bids and Awards Committee send their invitations to at least two observers, in addition to the representative of COA, in all stages of the procurement activity. Invitations should be sent at least five (5) days before the procurement activity as directed in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and

Disbursement vouchers for the payment of various goods and infrastructure projects should be supported with a copy of the invitation to observers.

The BAC post in the PhilGEPS website the Notices of Award and Notices to Proceed for procurement done through public bidding and all instances of alternative methods of procurement, except for contracts with Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) of ₱50,000.00 and below; and

The Municipal Accountant ensure that the printout copy of postings of the Notices of Awards and Notices to Proceed in the PhilGEPS website is included among the supporting documents in the payment of procured goods, services and infrastructure projects.

Determine first the objectives to be achieved in incurring certain expenditures, identify the factors affecting the expenditures, be it price or any established limits and find alternatives or ways to reduce expenditures to minimum without sacrificing the attainment of the objectives; and

Ensure that only reasonable expenditures are incurred and strictly follow the maximum allowable Daily Travelling Expenses of government personnel as prescribed under EO No. 77.

Require the accountable officers to renew their fidelity bonds before the expiration pursuant to Section 305 (f) of RA No. 7160 and Section 4.10 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009; and

The accountable officers to: (a) refrain from drawing cash advances and perform collection functions during the period of expiration of the fidelity bond; and (b) immediately apply for the renewal of the fidelity bond to safeguard from risk of loss of government funds due to unforeseen circumstances.

We recommended to direct the Municipal Treasurer to render his accounts and submit the required financial transactions together with the supporting documents to the Accounting Office, and the Municipal Accountant to submit the accounts and reports to COA on or before the 10th day of the succeeding month.

Likewise, the accountable officer and/or other officials concerned are reminded of the sanctions imposed under Section 122 of PD No. 1445 in relation to Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code on the automatic suspension of payment of salaries for failure to render accounts in a period of two months.

We recommended to prepare and cause the immediate submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) for COA AAR for CY 2022 using the attached prescribed format, henceforth observe strict compliance to COA Memorandum No. 2014-002 dated March 18, 2014.

We recommended to encourage the involvement of barangay officials in the tax campaign to reach out to a wider area and intensify tax collections thru formulating strategies to improve collection of real property taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and all revenue collectors to submit the consolidated and individual Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to the Office of the Auditor not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month pursuant to Sections 50 and 51 of the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator comply strictly with the requirements of Sections 2.1 and 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 on the submission of the required list of on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year as well as the posting of signboards to facilitate monitoring and validation of the Municipality’s awarded and implemented projects.

We recommended that management encourage the JO and contractual personnel to register as SSS self-employed members in adherence to the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-83 in order to avail of the benefits and protection against hazards and disability, sickness, maternity, old age, death and other contingencies that the SSS offers.

Direct the BAC Secretariat to submit copies of all contracts/purchase orders irrespective of amounts, and each and every supporting documents forming part thereof to the Audit Team within five working days from the date of execution or issuance thereof as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; and

Instruct the General Services Officer to notify in writing and furnished the Auditor concerned with copies of Acceptance and Inspection Receipt (AIR) within 24 hours after acceptance of deliveries of goods and services in accordance with Item 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002 dated May 18, 2009.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Bids and Awards Committee and the Municipal Engineer properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to development projects to pave the way for their timely implementation and completion;
Management to (i) implement immediately all the projects under the continuing appropriations, otherwise, unimplemented PPAs which are no longer feasible should be considered for immediate realignment subject to budgetary rules or reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% Development Fund for reprogram/re-appropriation to other development projects; (ii) strictly utilize the 20 percent DF only for those priority programs and projects prescribed under Section 3.2 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020; and (iii) revert to  the 20 percent DF the amount of ₱200,548.50 representing disbursements for non-development projects, which shall be re-appropriated to finance only those development programs, projects and activities allowable to be charged against said Fund; and

The LCE require the coordination between the Municipal Accountant and the designated General Services Officer towards the full implementation of the perpetual inventory method in the recording of all Inventory Accounts to ensure proper accounting and promote better control in the receipt and issuance of supplies and materials; and
The Municipal Accountant and designated General Services Officer maintain the prescribed ledger cards and stock cards, respectively, for each type of Inventory, keep track the actual Supplies Inventory on hand and reconcile the balances of the two inventory records.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the MDRRM Council to prepare and submit the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Investment Plan (LDRRMIP) indicating the: (i) detailed PPAs and the corresponding appropriation classified as either MOOE or Capital Outlay; and (ii) under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years for ease of monitoring of project implementation and evaluation;
The MDRRMC ensure that the LDRRMF be strictly utilized for the implementation of projects and activities for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and recovery; and
The Municipal Accountant to: (i) comply with the monthly submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF; and (ii) transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE for the current and previous years within the five-year validity period to the Special Trust Fund using the “Trust Liability-DRRMF” account in the Trust Fund books.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the BAC to undertake procurement in bulk to save time, costs and manpower hours using public bidding as the primary mode of procurement and discontinue the practice of procuring supplies and equipment through small value procurement unless the competitive bidding cannot be ultimately pursued, which should be justified by the Bids and Awards Committee and appropriate end-user or relevant office.

We recommended and Management agreed that payments for goods and services delivered as well as other operating expenses be made directly to the suppliers pursuant to the provisions of Section 93 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. The Municipal Treasurer limit the payments through reimbursement/cash advance and the Municipal Accountant deduct/withhold the mandatory taxes pursuant to Section 4.114.2 (a) of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 13-2018 dated March 15, 2018.
We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) undertake all procurements to pass through the regular mode of procurement, as possible, to promote competitive prices in accordance with RA No. 9184.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Sangguniang Bayan immediately pass a resolution authorizing the Municipal Treasurer to open separate depository accounts for the LDRRM Fund, 20 percent Development Fund and Loan funds;
The Municipal Treasurer facilitate the transfer of fund balances of each special account into their respective depository accounts; and
The Municipal Accountant maintain a special accounts subsidiary ledger and prepare a separate trial balance and financial statements for each special account at the end of each year, which shall form part of the schedules of the General Fund trial balance.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer and all revenue collectors to submit the consolidated and individual Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to the Office of the Auditor not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month pursuant to Sections 50 and 51 of the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that Management: Require the Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical inventory of all PPE items, prepare the required the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and a copy of which be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of the following year pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I; PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards for each group of PPEs shall be maintained by the Accounting Office and General Services Office, respectively; and The OIC-Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to set up the correct depreciation reckoned from the date of acquisition/completion of the assets up to the present by debiting prior period adjustment and crediting accumulated depreciation and ensure monthly recognition of depreciation expense of all depreciable PPE accounts;

We recommended that Management: Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments to recognize a liability representing the unutilized balance of the BGCM fund amounting to ₱819.56 and inappropriate charges amounting to ₱25,085.80 or a total of ₱25,905.36 and maintain a separate subsidiary ledger in compliance with LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020; Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the required documents to the Office of the Auditor for further evaluation of the transactions pursuant to Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020; Replenish the amount of ₱25,085.80 representing inappropriate charges against the BGCM fund which shall form part of the unutilized balance for re-appropriation to other allowable PPAs for COVID-19 response, otherwise, the remaining balance, if any, shall be reverted to the National Treasury after the lifting of the State of Calamity; Ensure that only authorized COVID-19 related PPAs shall be charged against the BGCM fund as enumerated under DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125 dated April 7, 2020; and Require the BAC to strictly comply with the posting requirement of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 for information purposes and to promote transparency and accountability in the conduct of Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act.

We recommended that Management: Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to undertake a detailed reconciliation/analysis of the accounts and investigate causes of errors/discrepancies for the purpose of booking up the necessary adjustments to arrive at the correct balances of the accounts; and Require the Accounting Office to maintain subsidiary ledgers (SL) for easy monitoring/tracing of accounts and a periodic reconciliation to their respective general ledger (GL) balances or controlling accounts shall be made at the end of every month to avoid existence of negative/abnormal balances pursuant to Section 111 of PD 1445 and NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended the following to the Management: Require the concerned accountable officers to settle their cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002, especially at year-end, for the timely recognition of expenses in the books of accounts; Direct the Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to officers and employees unless the previous cash advance is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made; The OIC-Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances by sending demand letters regularly and withhold the payment of any money due them in case of their failure to settle the liquidation of cash advances despite demand; and The OIC-Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and accountable officers ensure completeness of documents and payroll bear complete signatures of payees and proper approval before disbursements/liquidations are made.

We recommended that Management: Direct the General Services Office (GSO) to implement control over the use of government vehicle through properly accomplished and duly approved and serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets, a summary of which shall be made at the end of each month in a Monthly Report of Official Travels for audit purposes; and Require the GSO to prepare and submit the Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels to the Office of the Auditor for verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of the fuel consumed during the period.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit with the authorized depository bank the full amount of her collections daily or not later than the next banking day and discontinue the practice of retaining/withholding part of her collections pursuant to Section 69 (1) and (4) of P.D. 1445 and Section 32 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, to ensure the safety and avoid risk of loss of government funds.

We recommended that Management direct the OIC, Municipal Accountant to present the current and non-current portion of the Loans Payable-Domestic account and properly disclose the said accounts in the Notes to the Financial Statements in order to provide information to the users and for a fair presentation of these accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that The Municipal Government of Lezo strictly enforce the mandatory posting of warranty security by the contractors to guarantee that the contractor shall perform his responsibilities as prescribed in Section 62.2.3.1 of the IRR of RA 9184 and Section 9.1.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and The Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant maintain a list of completed projects as a reference for posting the required warranty security.

We recommended that Management fully institutionalize the MDRRMO by filling up the statutory/mandatory positions required under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) 2014-1 to assure implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and management.

The existence of its Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) with a net carrying value of ₱55.533 million as of December 31, 2018 could not be ascertained due to the failure of the Municipality to complete the inventory taking of its PPE and to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE), required under Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs, Volume 1, thus, casting doubts on the accuracy and correctness of the reported balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that: The Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to complete the conduct the physical inventory of the Municipality’s PPE and submit to the Audit Team the RPCPPE; The RPCPPE shall be reconciled with the accounting records in order to ensure that the PPE account balances are fairly presented in the financial statements. The discrepancies should be investigated and necessary adjustments should be made to come up with a complete and accurate balance of asset accounts, PPE in the financial statements; The Accounting Office should religiously maintain the PPELC to provide a detailed information of the PPE which can be of great importance in the conduct of the physical inventory; The Municipal Treasurer as the Property Custodian of the Municipality maintain Property Cards and provide/attach property sticker to each PPE item for ease of reference and property tagging; Henceforth, inventory taking and reconciliation of related subsidiary records should be regularly undertaken and regarded with utmost importance.

Some depreciable Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) amounting to ₱53.927 million were not provided with depreciation contrary to PPSAS 17 and COA Circular 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 resulting to overstatement of PPE accounts and changes in net assets/equity as reflected in the Statement of Financial Position and understatement of depreciation expenses as reflected in the Statement of Financial Performance, thus, affected the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management: Require the Municipal Accountant to compute the correct depreciation expenses of its assets and a journal entry must be drawn to update/correct the accumulated depreciation by a debit to depreciation expense (for the current year depreciation), debit to prior year’s adjustment (for the prior year’s depreciation) and a credit to accumulated depreciation (for the total unrecorded depreciation); Exert effort through the General Services Office/Office of the Municipal Treasurer, to reconstruct and update its property records and reconcile with the accounting records to facilitate the setting up and periodic provision of depreciation for those depreciable assets not provided with depreciation; Create an appraisal committee to conduct evaluation of assets whose records could not be found in order to establish the commencement of depreciation of the subjected assets and determine their depreciable cost and estimated remaining life for the computation of their respective depreciation; and Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant see to it that all depreciable properties owned by the LGU are provided with depreciation in accordance with the above-mentioned rules and regulations to ensure its fair presentation in the financial statements.

The Accounts Receivable, Other Receivables, Due from NGAs, Notes Receivables and Due from LGUs amounting to ₱1.409 million which remained dormant in the books for more than 10 years were not acted upon to facilitate the proper write-off of these accounts, resulting in the overstatement of agency assets contrary to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, thereby affecting the fair presentation of the financial statements.

Cash advances to officers and employees amounting to ₱267,037.25 remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2018 contrary to Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, resulting to accumulation and understated the total expenses and overstated the government equity and total receivables, thus affecting its fair presentation in the financial statements.

Copies of perfected contracts for infrastructure projects and delivery of goods and services together with supporting documents amounting to ₱53.897 million were not submitted to the Office of the Auditor within five working days upon execution as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, thus hampered the timely auditorial review of the said contracts.

The Municipality of Lezo did not fully implement and not able to complete on time its proposed projects/programs/activities totaling ₱16.026 million funded under the 20% Development Fund, contrary to Sections 287 and 332 of the Local Government Code and DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, thus, defeating the purpose for which the fund was established and depriving the intended beneficiaries of the vital services and benefits that could have been derived from the implementation of these projects.

The Municipality did not prepare and submit a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP and LDRRMIP resulting to delayed project implementation as indicated in the low percentage of fund utilization and accumulated unutilized balance of ₱5.025 million as of December 31, 2018, thus affecting the capacity of the local government units to build the disaster resilience of communities and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities.

Driver’s Trip Tickets were not properly and completely accomplished and the required Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels were not prepared and submitted to the Office of the Auditor contrary to COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 prescribing the use of the “Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles” thus, the reasonableness of the gasoline, oil and lubricants consumed totaling ₱0.600 million could not be ascertained.

The Municipal Planning and Development Office has not yet established a GAD Database contrary to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01, thus depriving the Municipality of the vital information for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.

The Municipality of Lezo did not fill up the two mandatory positions prescribed for the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (MDRRMO), hence implementation of actions and measures pertaining to disaster risk reduction and management may not be assured.

Contractual and Job Order employees were not provided with Self-Employment Coverage under the Social Security System (SSS), thereby depriving them of the entitlement to retirement benefits and other financial assistances that the SSS offers.

Funds transferred to NGOs/POs amounting to ₱70,000.00 were recorded as a debit to the account title Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses (5-02-99-990) instead of Due from Non-Government Organizations/People's Organizations (1-03-06-030) contrary to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, thus, monitoring of the utilization of the fund could not be made and resulted to improper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

The evaluation on the efficiency and effectiveness of the implementation of the Municipality’s GAD programs/projects/activities during the year cannot be properly effected because the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and GAD Accomplishment Report were not prepared in accordance with the pertinent provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01, hence, rendered it difficult to determine if expenses charged were GAD related or can be categorized as such.

Allocation of one percent of the Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) for the strengthening and implementation of the projects and activities of the Local Councils for the Protection of Children (LCPC) as prescribed under Section 15 of R.A. 9344 was underbudgeted by ₱0.542 million and the Municipality was not able to formulate a comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention and prepare the LCPC Annual Work and Financial Plan, thereby affecting the adequate implementation of the programs, projects and other social services pertinent to the protection and welfare of all the children.

The operation of the Lezo RHU Basic Emergency Obstetrics and Newborn Care (BEmONC) Facility is not covered by an ordinance authorizing the same to collect user fees as provided for in Section 132 of R.A. No. 7160 and Chapter V of Department of Health (DOH) Department Memorandum No. 2009-0110 dated May 10, 2009 thereby, wanting legal basis and deprived the LGU of the much-needed resources to operate and sustain a responsive (BEmONC) service facility within the municipality.

The existence, propriety and correctness of the balances of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) totaling ₱34.690 million could not be ascertained due to the failure of the management to conduct physical inventory of the account and to prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) as required in Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I, thus, affecting the fairness of presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the General Service Office Head/BAC Secretariat submit copy of all purchase orders/perfected contracts to the Office of the Auditor with five (5) working days from their perfection and each of all the documents forming part thereof as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

The Local Chief Executive (LCE) through the General Services Office shall implement control over the use of government vehicle through properly accomplished and duly approved and serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets, a summary of which shall be made at the end of each month in a Monthly Report of Official Travels for audit purposes; and

Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels shall likewise be prepared and submitted to the Office of the Auditor for verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of the fuel consumed during the period.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to promptly prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester in accordance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and furnish the Audit Team a printed copy of the PMR to provide basis for monitoring the status of procurement activities of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipality strengthen the capabilities of its Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to enable it to perform its mandated functions under Section 4, Rule 6, IRR of R.A. 10121 especially in the formulation of an LDRRM Plan/LDRRM Investment Plan as guide in the utilization the LDRRMF.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to file a request for write-off of the receivable account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected in accordance to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and
Diligent effort should be exerted to collect the remaining receivables by sending demand letters and effecting salary deductions from municipal employees and incumbent barangay officials.

We recommended that management require the liquidation of cash advances within the time frame required by law. The Municipal Accountant should stop the practice of granting additional cash advances unless the previous cash advances were fully liquidated.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances by sending demand letters regularly and withhold the payment of any money due them in case of their failure to settle the liquidation of cash advances despite demand. Likewise, the management institute appropriate measures/actions to enforce liquidations of cash advances in accordance with the afore-cited rules and regulations.

We recommended that the General Service Office Head/BAC Secretariat submit copy of all contracts to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from the date perfection and each of all the documents forming part thereof as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that:
The LDRRMO review and update annually the LDDRMF Investment Plan to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund; and
Municipality strengthen the capabilities of its Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to enable it to perform its mandated functions under Section 4, Rule 6, IRR of R.A. 10121 especially in the formulation of an LDRRM Plan/LDRRM Investment Plan as guide in the utilization the LDRRMF and proper monitoring of the fund.

We recommended that:

Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels shall likewise be prepared and submitted to the Office of the Auditor for verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of the fuel consumed during the period.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team the documentary requirements as required under COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 27, 2007 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for evaluation and review; and
Ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 27, 2007 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in releasing government funds to NGOs/POs.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to properly recognize fund transfers already made to NGOs/POs by debiting Due from NGOs/POs (1-03-06-330) and a credit to Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses (5-02-99-990);
Management require the implementing NGOs/POs to submit Fund Utilization Report on the Implementation of the funds received from the Municipal Government; and
Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the proper recording and classification of the financial transactions in the financial statements of the local government unit as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to prepare and submit the GAD Plan and Budget to the PPDO of the Provincial Government to ensure the alignment of the municipal GAD PPAs with the priorities of the province and then from the PPDO to the DILG Regional Offices for its review and approval; and
Submit GAD Accomplishment Report duly reviewed by the DILG with all the required attachments enumerated in C.8 of Item 4 of the JMC to enable us to properly evaluate whether the planned programs/projects/activities and the GAD reported utilizations were all gender-responsive and addresses the gender issues of the Municipality’s constituents.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the establishment of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other information and to coordinate with the Municipal GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and the Local DILG Field Officer on the generation and processing of the data in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01;
Ensure the effective implementation of the Municipal GAD Database that contains systematically gathered and regularly updated information relevant to gender responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and
Ensure that adequate budget is provided for the establishment and maintenance of the GAD Database.

Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the establishment of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other information and to coordinate with the Municipal GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and the Local DILG Field Officer on the generation and processing of the data in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01;
Ensure the effective implementation of the Municipal GAD Database that contains systematically gathered and regularly updated information relevant to gender responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and
Ensure that adequate budget is provided for the establishment and maintenance of the GAD Database.

We recommended that the MSWMB expedite the finalization and submission for approval to the DENR of the 10-year SWMP of the Municipality that will serve as a guide in the efficient and effective implementation of the Municipality’s solid waste management programs and activities, i.e. segregation at source, segregated collection, establishment of MRF and other activities that will ensure the protection of public health and environment that may also generate funds for the Municipality that will eventually help in the payment of garbage collection services.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit her collections regularly and intact and discontinue the practice of retaining/withholding part of collections pursuant to Section 69(1) and (4) of P.D. 1445 and Section 32 of New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), to ensure the safety and avoid risk of loss of government funds.

We recommended that the General Service Office Head/BAC Secretariat submit copy of all purchase orders/perfected contracts to the Office of the Auditor with five (5) working days from their perfection and each of all the documents forming part thereof as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all concerned department heads to submit their action plans and status of implementation on the CY 2015 AAR observations and recommendations and information gathered therein be used to properly accomplish the AAPSI, and submit the same to the COA Auditor.

We further recommended that the municipality submit the duly accomplished Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) Form within sixty (60) days from the receipt of the Annual Audit Report

We recommended that:
The Local Chief Executive instruct the LDRRM Officer, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Budget Officer to see to it that the reports pertaining to the 5% LDRRMF are reconciled to avoid its unreliability;
The LDRRMO review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund; and
The Municipal Government fully implement the LDRRMF programs and see to it that all expenses incurred are in accordance with the approved budget /Annual Investment Plan to strengthen the capacity of the local government unit to build the disaster resilience of communities and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.

We recommend that payments on infrastructure projects shall be supported with the required documents in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012. We also recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure the completeness of bidding documents necessary for the conduct of bid evaluation in conformity with the prescribed procurement procedures set forth under R.A. 9184 and its Revised IRR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that management:

Stop the charging and payment of personal services, operating expenses and other expenses of the district office which are not included in the list of priority expenses chargeable against SEF.

Make proper representation for the payment of expenditures for other important operational expenses of public schools but not included in the priority items where SEF may be used and the same should be properly charged to the General Fund of the Municipal Government subject to the conditions provided under Section 458 (xi) of RA 7160 or to the appropriate budget of the Department of Education, Culture and Sports (DECS).

Ensure that the Special Education Fund be expended solely and exclusively for expenditures provided for under Republic Act No. 5447, Republic Act No. 7160 and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos.  01 s. 1998 and 01-A dated April 14, 1998 and March 14, 2000, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accounting Office exhaust all possible means to identify the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to effect immediate adjustment in the books of accounts.  Likewise, periodic reconciliation of the accounts should be made, to ensure reliability and accuracy of the recorded financial transactions.

We recommend that management:

Coordinate with the Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG) and the Philippine Commission on Women (PCW) for the design/implementation of courses relative to capacity development on gender mainstreaming, gender responsive planning/budgeting, gender analysis/assessment, development/utilization of GAD tools and institutionalization of GAD database.

Take determined efforts to put in place the institutional mechanisms/procedures for pursuing gender mainstreaming in the municipality as provided under Section 4.0 of the PCW/DILG/DBM/NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-1 such as the establishment/strengthening of the GAD Focal Point System, setting up/maintenance of GAD Database and mainstreaming of GAD concerns.

We recommend the following:

a. Create an inventory committee that will conduct physical inventory and come up with the inventory report of PPE;  

b. Require each department head to submit an inventory report of all PPE found in their respective offices to the designated supply officer which will be validated by the inventory committee, to facilitate the immediate inventory taking.  The outcome of the Physical Inventory report should then be reconciled with the books of accounts; and 
 
c. Require the issuance of an Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for all procured or acquired properties which shall be used to report on the receipt of property and equipment for official use from the Designated Property Officer.

Henceforth, the General Services Officer/Acting Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant should exert extra effort to update and reconcile their records regularly.  Any discrepancies should be investigated and necessary adjustments should be made to come up with a complete and accurate balance of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
